title: Wine And Roses

jokes.text: The phone-chain chaos as Johnny tries to reach Herb Ertlinger through the motel — Stevie patches it through, David has headphones in, Alexis answers and tells him he has the wrong room, the call keeps getting lost
jokes.setup: Johnny is waiting for an important business call that Stevie is trying to patch through
jokes.punchline: The call cascades through incompetent hands — David ignores it, Alexis tells the caller he has the wrong room and hangs up — the entire family can't manage a single phone transfer
jokes.explanation: The extended phone-chain sequence works beautifully as an opening set piece — it's fundamentally about how this once-powerful family can't manage basic motel logistics. Each beat escalates the failure. David with headphones in, Alexis cheerfully telling the caller he has the wrong room and hanging up — both perfectly characterize the kids' oblivious self-absorption. High character integration makes this more than a generic farce.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Hello? Yes, hello, this is Johnny Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, I'm here regarding the wine distribution agreement.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, you're breaking up. Can you hear me?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, the connection is terrible. Hold on.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Hello? Hello? I think we've lost them.

jokes.text: Alexis answers the phone for Johnny's important business call and tells Herb Ertlinger 'Sorry, you have the wrong room. He's actually in room 6' then hangs up with 'No problem. Bye.'
jokes.setup: Johnny desperately needs Alexis to hand off the call to him
jokes.punchline: Alexis, cheerfully oblivious, redirects the caller and hangs up as if doing everyone a favour
jokes.explanation: This is the standout beat of the opening sequence. 'No problem. Bye.' is delivered with Alexis's signature breezy, helpful-but-catastrophically-unhelpful energy. It's entirely her: she genuinely thinks she handled it well. The economy is exceptional — the punchline is three words. It's character-specific to the point where no other character in the show could deliver it this way. Justifies the 8+ score.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I need you to transfer this call. It's very important.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay, hold on. I'll just... put you on hold and then...

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, no, no, you have to press the blinking light first!

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Which one is blinking?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: They're all blinking! They're all blinking!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well now you've pressed too many! You've killed the whole system!

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's just a phone.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It is NOT just a phone, Stevie! This is a multi-line telecommunications device!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I once managed the switchboard at a four-star resort in Sochi. This should be simple.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Did you?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No. But I watched someone do it once from very far away.

jokes.text: David: 'Can you get a heart murmur from a lack of sleep? Because I have not slept since we got here and I think my body is shutting down.'
jokes.setup: New scene opens with David in apparent distress
jokes.punchline: He immediately catastrophizes — going from insomnia to heart murmur to 'body shutting down' in one breath
jokes.explanation: Classic David Rose hypochondria establishing the episode's B-plot. The escalation from 'can't sleep' to 'body shutting down' is very him. Works as character comedy but it's the first beat of a running gag, so it's setting up more than delivering on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Hi, yes, hello? I'm sorry, I think you have the wrong number.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, there's no one here by that name. You've definitely reached the wrong place.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Alexis! Who is that?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I don't know, Dad. They're looking for some guy.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Well, I hate to admit it, but I actually know very little about heart murmurs.' David: 'Well, I have one.'
jokes.setup: David is spiraling about his health and Alexis is his only available listener
jokes.punchline: Alexis's frank admission that she 'hates to admit' ignorance about heart murmurs — as if it's unusual for her not to know — followed by David's bald assertion that he has one
jokes.explanation: The phrasing 'I hate to admit it' implies Alexis normally WOULD know — as if her knowledge base is vast but this specific area is a gap. That's a tiny, perfect Alexis joke. David's flat 'Well, I have one' as a medical self-diagnosis is also good deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I think something's seriously wrong with me. I have a sharp pain in my side, my throat feels tight, and I'm fairly certain my heart is about to give out.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm calling 911. This is it. This is how it ends.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait. I just realized I'm fine. I think I just needed to eat something.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Honestly David, I have tried to find my pulse like a thousand times and nothing. So don't worry about it.'
jokes.setup: David insists his pulse is irregular
jokes.punchline: Alexis's attempt at reassurance — she's tried to find her OWN pulse many times and couldn't, therefore he shouldn't worry — is logically backwards and inadvertently suggests she might also be in peril
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely clever joke. The reassurance is structurally backwards — 'I can't find my pulse either so don't worry' implies either that pulses are undetectable or that Alexis is also possibly dead. The fact that she says it with complete sincerity as comfort makes it perfect Alexis characterization. High character integration — only she would offer this as solace.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I don't know, I've never been sick before.

jokes.text: David's list of grievances escalates to 'I have not been able to find kale anywhere here' as evidence his body is shutting down
jokes.setup: David is listing symptoms of his apparent cardiac emergency
jokes.punchline: The inability to find kale is included as a medical symptom on equal footing with heart murmurs and immune system failure
jokes.explanation: The kale line works as both a health joke and a fish-out-of-water joke — David's SoHo lifestyle incompatibilities are being reframed as medical crises. The escalating list structure sets this up beautifully; the kale is a perfect comedic non-sequitur that's also entirely in character.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can't find my pulse. I think something's wrong with me.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I can never find mine either.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, so you're fine.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'How long are we gonna be talking about you for?'
jokes.setup: David is in the middle of a panicked health monologue
jokes.punchline: Alexis cuts him off not to offer help but to redirect attention — entirely self-interested but framed casually
jokes.explanation: Five words that say everything about Alexis. Maximum economy. It's funny because it treats David's potential cardiac event as merely monopolizing her time rather than a concern. Slightly less original than the pulse joke but still very effective.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have a very long list of health concerns, and I've decided to address them all at once.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, I have health concerns too! I think I might be gluten intolerant.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, I'm in the middle of a health crisis.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, but this is serious. I had a piece of bread yesterday and I felt a little bloated.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Well, I'm gonna go for a jog, so don't die until I get back.' David: 'Well, how long are you going for?' Alexis: 'Um, I don't really know. How long are you gonna be like this for?'
jokes.setup: David believes he is dying; Alexis is leaving for a jog
jokes.punchline: Alexis negotiates the timing of his death against her exercise schedule — and then turns it into a negotiation about how long his symptoms will last, as if scheduling an appointment
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best exchanges. 'Don't die until I get back' is dark and funny, but the follow-up exchange is what elevates it: David asking how long her jog is, and Alexis turning it back on him ('How long are you gonna be like this for?') as if his medical emergency is an inconvenience to be scheduled around. The callback structure within a two-line exchange is elegant. High character integration — this sibling dynamic is specific and earned.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You're not having a heart attack. You're like, what, 40?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, so you're fine.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'm experiencing chest pains, so I think I should go to the hospital.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's probably just anxiety.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm having a heart attack, Alexis!

jokes.text: David: 'Don't be a little b...' Alexis: 'Bye!' David: 'When I'm in this state!'
jokes.setup: David is about to level an insult at Alexis as she leaves
jokes.punchline: Alexis cuts him off mid-insult with a cheerful 'Bye!' leaving David to finish his complaint to an empty room
jokes.explanation: Good comedic timing beat — Alexis's perfectly timed 'Bye!' cutting off David's profanity is a well-structured reaction. The truncated insult and David's wounded 'When I'm in this state!' adds pathos-comedy. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I could be in the hospitel right now, Alexis. The hospitel. Where they don't know if you're going to make it or if you're just going to get better and go home.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's just a hospital.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, it's a hospitel.

jokes.text: Johnny and Moira at the winery tasting — 'There's something in mine.' Herb: 'Oh, that's a ladybug. That's one of the good ones.'
jokes.setup: They're tasting wine at a winery that proudly uses no insecticides
jokes.punchline: An insect has made it into the wine; Herb reassures them it's 'one of the good ones' — as if there's a hierarchy of acceptable insects in your glass
jokes.explanation: Beautifully earned — the no-insecticide setup pays off immediately with an actual insect in the glass. Herb's unruffled 'that's one of the good ones' is perfect deadpan business-owner comedy. Economy is exceptional: four words ('that's one of the good ones') conveys the entire joke. Highly quotable. The setup and payoff are elegant.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: There's a bug in my wine!

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Oh, that's one of the good ones.

jokes.text: Moira: 'No, this tastes like something one should not put in their mouth.'
jokes.setup: They are tasting Herb's fruit wine, which is apparently unpleasant
jokes.punchline: Moira's elaborate circumlocution — 'something one should not put in their mouth' — is both evasive and devastatingly damning
jokes.explanation: Very Moira. The grandiloquent phrasing of what could simply be 'this is disgusting' — using passive construction and vague horror — is exactly her register. The indirection is both funnier and more insulting than a direct complaint would be. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This is... a beverage. Of sorts. It tastes like someone filtered a fruit salad through a gym sock and then aged it in a portapotty. I'm getting notes of desperation, a hint of regret, and a lingering aftertaste of poor life choices. Not mine, obviously. Someone else's. Many someones, actually. This wine doesn't have a bouquet—it has a full aromatherapy crisis. I've tasted paint thinner with more complexity and charm. The only thing this pairs well with is the existential dread of knowing I voluntarily put this in my mouth.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'It's next to a landfill, John.' Moira: 'He said former landfill.'
jokes.setup: Moira is skeptical about Johnny's winery business proposition
jokes.punchline: Johnny defends the location by citing Herb's preferred euphemism 'former landfill' — as if the distinction makes all the difference
jokes.explanation: A tight, efficient joke about the absurdity of corporate euphemisms. Johnny is clearly grasping at reasons this is viable and 'former landfill' is the best he's got. The correction is both defensive and self-aware about how thin the defense is.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, actually, it's quite lovely. There are notes of elderberry and a hint of oak.

jokes.text: Herb presents his 'Cabernet Merlot petit grenache.' Moira: 'Sounds delightfully busy.'
jokes.setup: Herb presents a wine blend with a very crowded name
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'sounds delightfully busy' — a faux-compliment that is actually a polite dismay at the overcrowded nomenclature
jokes.explanation: Three words that do a lot of work. 'Delightfully busy' is quintessentially Moira — using theatrical language to deliver an elegant put-down that the recipient can't quite identify as negative. Strong economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm fine. I just looked it up online, and apparently I have six months to live.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Like it? It's fruit wine. What's not to like?' — said after she previously called it something one should not put in one's mouth
jokes.setup: Herb asks if Moira likes the wine enough to endorse it
jokes.punchline: Moira pivots to enthusiastic endorsement of a wine she described as disgusting minutes earlier, now that she's being offered a role
jokes.explanation: The callback to her earlier disgust makes this a proper earned joke. 'What's not to like?' is delivered with complete sincerity despite everything we just witnessed. It's a sharp character moment — Moira's ambition immediately overrides her aesthetic standards. The reversal is clean and well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So what you're telling me is that if I were to get sick, my only option would be to either drive an hour to the nearest hospital, or just... die?

jokes.text: David: 'According to Web MD, I'm having a pulmonary embolism, which is much, much worse.' — his condition has been upgraded from heart attack
jokes.setup: Opening of the scene, Twyla asks about his earlier health scare
jokes.punchline: He's researched further and promoted his own diagnosis to something more serious
jokes.explanation: The escalation of self-diagnosis from heart murmur → heart attack → pulmonary embolism tracks perfectly with hypochondriac logic. 'Which is much, much worse' is delivered with relish rather than fear. Good running gag escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, the nearest hospital is about forty-five minutes away, so if you are having a heart attack, you're basically done for.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm joking. You're fine.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'Well, you look good.' David: 'That's... just the way I look.'
jokes.setup: Twyla tries to reassure David that he seems fine
jokes.punchline: David dismisses this as evidence of anything because his healthy appearance is merely a fixed attribute, not a sign of health
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. Four words dismissing a reasonable observation. David's vanity and his hypochondria are simultaneously expressed — he looks good not because he's fine but because he simply always does. Very quotable for its compact absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: David, I'm not going to sugarcoat this. You're going to die.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm kidding. You're fine.

jokes.text: David: 'People just drop dead in the streets out of sheer neglect?'
jokes.setup: Twyla tells him the only hospital is in the next town over
jokes.punchline: David imagines a hellscape of medical neglect as the logical endpoint of rural healthcare
jokes.explanation: David's aristocratic horror at provincial life expressed through hyperbolic catastrophizing. 'Out of sheer neglect' is a good phrase — it implies indifference rather than lack of resources, which is the more insulting read. Solidly in David's voice.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Have you ever done any drugs?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Have I ever... done any drugs? Ted, I grew up in a penthouse.

jokes.text: Doctor: 'if it is a heart attack we're way too far away from the nearest hospital for you to make it through the night.' David: 'What?' Doctor: 'Kidding.'
jokes.setup: David is at the doctor, genuinely convinced he's dying
jokes.punchline: The doctor baits him with a worst-case scenario and then immediately says 'kidding' — a medical professional trolling an anxious patient
jokes.explanation: The doctor using David's anxiety against him is a good inversion — we expect doctors to be reassuring. The single-word 'Kidding' after the terrifying pronouncement is excellent economy. The deadpan confidence of the delivery would land hard.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So what you're saying is that I'm having a panic attack because my brain is broken?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, no, your brain isn't broken. It's just reacting to stress.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's very comforting, Ted. In Hollywood, we just call that 'being sensitive' and charge people $500 an hour to listen to us talk about it.

jokes.text: Doctor: 'Now, have you been around any feces in the past two days?' David: 'I don't n...' Doctor: 'So, no.'
jokes.setup: The doctor is going through a diagnostic checklist
jokes.punchline: Before David can answer, the doctor reads his reaction — or his type — and answers for him: 'So, no.' It's both a feces joke and a class joke
jokes.explanation: The structure is tight: the question is unexpected and slightly absurd, David's flustered half-response, and the doctor's pre-emptive 'So, no' tells us everything about how David reads in a rural context. The answer implies the doctor knows David's type from appearance alone. Efficient and funny.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, Ted, where do I even begin? I went from having a walk-in closet the size of most people's homes to sharing a room with my brother. I used to have a sommelier. Now I have a gas station attendant recommending wine pairings. My therapist was on speed dial. Now my therapist is a local real estate agent who gives unsolicited life advice while showing me affordable housing options I cannot afford.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So yes, Ted, I would say the transition has been... stressful.

jokes.text: I don't n— [reaction beat: clearly 'no'] — So, no.
jokes.setup: The doctor asks if David has been around feces.
jokes.punchline: David starts a horrified denial and the doctor reads the reaction and just moves on, filing it as a clean 'no.' The implication is David's reaction is so visceral the words aren't needed.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the feces question — the doctor just reading David's sputtering horror as confirmation — is a well-timed visual beat. The economy of 'So, no.' is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was in a very dark place. I lost everything. My family's company collapsed, we lost all our money, and I had to start my life completely over from scratch.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh wow. Did you try Folgers?

jokes.text: David: 'I went from living in a 2,500 square foot SoHo live/workspace to a motel room with my sister.' Doctor: 'Wow. Eek. That'll do it.'
jokes.setup: The doctor asks if anything stressful has happened lately
jokes.punchline: David's recounting of his fall, combined with the doctor's extremely casual 'Eek. That'll do it' — treating it as a perfectly sufficient clinical explanation
jokes.explanation: The doctor's 'Wow. Eek.' is a great response — it's the most un-clinical reaction possible, and it validates David's horror while being absurdly casual about it. Also functions as a good summary of the show's premise delivered by a stranger.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Look, I may have said it was liquid stink, but that doesn't mean it doesn't have its merits.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What merits?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, it's... it's certainly a liquid.

jokes.text: Moira repeatedly trying and failing to deliver the Herb Ertlinger winery ad copy — fumbling 'Herb Ertlinger' into various non-words
jokes.setup: Moira is practicing the commercial script alone in her room
jokes.punchline: Even in private rehearsal, she can't land the name — 'Herb Ertlinger's fruity cab-' trails off in defeat
jokes.explanation: Sets up the running gag of Moira's inability to manage the name. Effective as a plant for the eventual payoff. Less funny on its own than as setup.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What is this? 'Notes of elderberry with a hint of—' this isn't real! These aren't real words! You can't just make up wine terminology and expect people to believe it!

jokes.text: Johnny: '"Plummy bouquet"? Is this guy for real?' Moira: 'His turns of phrase leave much to be desired, but the wine is potable.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is reading Herb's script and finds the language absurd
jokes.punchline: Moira's measured 'the wine is potable' — a technical bar-clearer that doesn't actually constitute a compliment, and is a notable step back from 'What's not to like?'
jokes.explanation: 'Potable' is doing a lot of work — it means merely drinkable without being poisonous, which is the absolute minimum standard. Moira's vocabulary elevating the faintest praise is good character comedy. The callback to her earlier enthusiasm is implicit and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Herb, you're a moron.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, he may be a moron, but he's OUR moron. And he believed in us when no one else would.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'You called it liquid stink this morning.' Moira: 'The whites seem less stinky.'
jokes.setup: Johnny catches Moira softening her position on the wine
jokes.punchline: 'The whites seem less stinky' — a half-hearted partial concession that doesn't really walk back 'liquid stink' very convincingly
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'liquid stink' is introduced retroactively and immediately becomes the best description of the wine we've heard. 'The whites seem less stinky' as her recalibrated position is perfect — damning with faint praise while still holding the aesthetic ground. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The bag is so cheap it's genuinely repulsive to touch

jokes.text: Johnny holding up the Herb Ertlinger tote bag: 'Two-ply toilet paper has more heft.'
jokes.setup: Herb has sent along a branded tote bag as a gift/prop
jokes.punchline: Johnny's specific comparison to two-ply toilet paper — not just 'thin' but thinner than the absolute baseline of disposable products
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'two-ply toilet paper' elevates this above a generic thin-thing joke. The comparison object perfectly conveys both the cheapness of the bag and Johnny's instinct for product quality. Solid observational comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Why won't this just... fold?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's a bag. How is this so difficult?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Johnny, you're doing it wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not doing it wrong, the bag is doing it wrong!

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Are you kidding me? Come on! Hey, I haven't even given that guy a second's thought since I got home.'
jokes.setup: Moira asks if Johnny's negativity is because Herb didn't hire him
jokes.punchline: Johnny's overly emphatic denial — 'I haven't given that guy a second's thought' — is clearly a lie told in a way that makes its falseness obvious
jokes.explanation: The protest-too-much gag is a familiar comedic structure, but it's executed with reasonable character specificity for Johnny. The 'Are you kidding me? Come on!' opener shows he's been stewing. Functional but not one of the episode's stronger moments.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Why are you wearing a ball gown to help us move boxes?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, when Beyoncé did Coachella, she wore couture. So...

jokes.text: 'Peachafication!' Johnny: 'Who uses a word like "peachafication"?' [beat] 'A moron... would use that.' Moira: 'That moron is the first moron to offer me an acting role in a very long time.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is mocking Herb's absurd invented wine vocabulary
jokes.punchline: Moira's rebuttal lands on 'that moron is the first moron to offer me an acting role in a very long time' — acknowledging the moronism while defending the moron
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's emotional and comedic peaks. The word 'peachafication' is funny in itself, and Johnny's self-correcting 'a moron would use that' (catching himself insulting a potential partner) is good. But Moira's counter — accepting the moron designation and doubling it — while revealing her professional desperation with dignity intact, is beautifully written. 'That moron is the first moron' is perfect repetition for comic rhythm, and the pathos underneath it is what makes Schitt's Creek great. Justifies 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Why are you dressed like that to clean the toilets?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm taking fashion inspiration from Bella Hadid at the airport. You know, casual but make it fashion.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Bella Hadid was going somewhere nice though.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Exactly. And I'm going somewhere nice too. The sewage system.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I don't want to use the word "comeback," that's... I don't wanna jinx it, but...'
jokes.setup: Moira is describing what this fruit wine commercial could mean for her career
jokes.punchline: The studied avoidance of the word 'comeback' — as if naming it would destroy it — while obviously meaning exactly that
jokes.explanation: A tender moment with a comedic edge. The verbal dance around 'comeback' reveals Moira's vulnerability while being funny. The trailing '...but...' is a nice touch — she almost says it anyway. Works as both comedy and character development.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, you have to understand that fashion is about creating a narrative. It's about telling a story with your clothes.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I just... I wear what fits.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, but *how* it fits is the whole point. Like, this dress is very Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy meets early Gwyneth.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Who's Carolyn... Bessette?

jokes.text: Johnny and Moira picking up trash in their formal wear while Herb's tote bag is inspected — 'But seriously, how cheap ass is that bag?' Moira, ew-ing while putting trash in the bag
jokes.setup: The Roses are doing their assigned community clean-up while dressed in their signature finery
jokes.punchline: Moira shrieking 'Ew ew ew ew!' and struggling to pick up trash without opening her eyes — combined with the ongoing tote bag critique
jokes.explanation: The visual comedy of this scene — the Roses in Valentino picking up roadside trash — is a good fish-out-of-water set piece. 'You have to open your eyes to get it in the bag!' is a funny practical note. The Ew-chain is physical comedy supported by character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, when you have a very small wardrobe, you have very high expectations for each piece.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Oh honey, I'm just the money behind the scenes. Not that I don't have a few company commercials under my belt, but...'
jokes.setup: Moira says no one knows the business better than Johnny
jokes.punchline: Johnny's self-deprecating correction that he's 'just the money behind the scenes' — immediately undermined by his inability to resist mentioning his 'few company commercials'
jokes.explanation: A small Johnny character beat — his false modesty immediately cracks. The pause after 'but...' suggests he's stopping himself from going further. Modest laugh, good character work.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I've been really focused on achieving that rock-beaten freshness in my laundry.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Rock-beaten freshness? What does that even mean?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, I literally beat the laundry against rocks. It's a traditional cleaning method.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, no. We have a washing machine.

jokes.text: Moira on why she dresses up for community service: 'Naomi Campbell wore Dolce & Gabbana couture to her community service. And just because she was picking up roadkill did not mean that she needed to look like it. And I just always really admired her courage.'
jokes.setup: Stevie asks why Moira gets dressed up to pick up trash
jokes.punchline: Moira's Naomi Campbell anecdote delivered as a genuine inspirational story — 'I admired her courage' applied to wearing couture during court-mandated service
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best single speech. The Naomi Campbell reference is perfect Moira: she finds a celebrity precedent for everything, treats court-mandated community service as a lifestyle statement, and concludes with 'I admired her courage' — as if dressing well for forced labor is an act of bravery. It's completely sincere, completely Moira, and thoroughly absurd. High across all dimensions. Character integration near-perfect — this could only be Moira. Justifies 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I think we need to really focus on the blocking here. The camera needs to find its motivation.

dialogue.speaker: Director
dialogue.line: The camera doesn't have motivation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, everything has motivation. That's filmmaking 101. And I think we should do another take, but this time, let's really lean into the subtext of the cereal.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Um... I didn't understand a word you just said.'
jokes.setup: Moira has just delivered the Naomi Campbell speech
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat, honest, four-word assessment — the audience surrogate reaction to Moira's world
jokes.explanation: The perfect button to the Naomi Campbell speech. Stevie as deadpan audience surrogate works because she says what we're thinking but what no one in Moira's life would say. 'I didn't understand a word you just said' is the funniest possible response and the most honest. Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not having a panic attack, I'm just experiencing a heightened sense of awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh, well then I'm not being selfish, I'm just prioritizing my needs.

jokes.text: Stevie to Johnny: 'Besides, it's nice to... change clothes every once in a while. Something you might wanna consider doing at some point.'
jokes.setup: They're discussing why Moira dresses up for trash pickup
jokes.punchline: Stevie pivots to a casual dig at Johnny's wardrobe choices — entirely unprompted, delivered flatly
jokes.explanation: A mild dig from Stevie, who excels at casual insults. Works as a Stevie character beat. The delivery ('at some point') adds a nice indefinite quality — as if she's not sure he's capable of it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I need you to skip yoga tomorrow.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, I can't skip yoga. I have a standing appointment.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I have a standing appointment with my therapist, and I'm going to cancel it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, so cancel it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can't cancel it. I'm going to need you to cancel your yoga so that I don't have to cancel my therapy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That makes no sense.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It makes perfect sense. We have a system. Remember when I skipped my birthday party so you could go to that thing?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You skipped your own birthday party?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes! And now it's your turn to be selfless.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I change my clothes. They're just all the same.' Stevie: 'Uh-huh. Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Stevie has implied Johnny doesn't change his clothes
jokes.punchline: Johnny's defense — technically accurate (he does change, they're just identical) — is not really a defense, and Stevie's flat 'Uh-huh. Yeah.' acknowledges this without conceding
jokes.explanation: The logic of 'they're all the same' is genuinely funny — it's technically a rebuttal that proves the original point. Stevie's non-response is perfect. Tight, character-specific exchange.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm here to help with your commercial.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's very sweet, but I have a whole team.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I know production. I can offer valuable insights.

dialogue.speaker: Director
dialogue.line: And you are?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Her husband. I think the lighting is all wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Director
dialogue.line: We've been doing this for twenty years.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, clearly it shows.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: John, perhaps you could wait in the car.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: The blocking is atrocious. She should enter from stage left.

dialogue.speaker: Director
dialogue.line: Sir, I'm going to have to ask you to leave the set.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You're making a mistake.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: He's banned now, isn't he?

dialogue.speaker: Director
dialogue.line: Permanently.

jokes.text: After everyone reacts with alarm to Stevie beating laundry on rocks — cut to her motel room, which is unexpectedly warm and nice. Stevie: 'What? It's actually nice in here.'
jokes.setup: The Roses have been lamenting their terrible motel accommodations; Stevie is just the motel desk clerk
jokes.punchline: Stevie's room — which we've presumably never seen — is comfortable and normal, inverting the expectation that the whole building is miserable
jokes.explanation: A good visual misdirection. The audience expects more squalor; instead it's a reasonable room. The implication is that the Roses' misery is partially self-generated — the motel isn't that bad, they're just not Stevie. 'What? It's actually nice in here' delivered with mild defensiveness is good.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No way.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not doing partner yoga with you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Trust me, the feeling is mutual.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We can barely stand being in the same room.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I would rather do literally anything else.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Don't worry, they're fresh. They're clean.' David: 'Hmm. Ooh! Smell that rock-beaten freshness.'
jokes.setup: Stevie's admission that she beats laundry against rocks; David inspects the results
jokes.punchline: David's sarcastically enthusiastic 'Smell that rock-beaten freshness' — adopting an imaginary ad slogan for primitive laundry
jokes.explanation: David's fake advertising copy for rock laundry is a solid bit. 'Rock-beaten freshness' sounds like a real detergent line, which makes it funnier. Also a nice David/Stevie dynamic moment — their antagonistic-friendly energy is developing.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Roland couldn't make it today. He's been having some... digestive issues. But you know, it's fine. He'll be back next week.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'What? It's just I do beat my laundry against rocks.' David: 'You're a freak.'
jokes.setup: David has made a joke about 'rock-beaten freshness' and Stevie clarifies she actually does this
jokes.punchline: The revelation that the sarcastic joke was literally true — and David's flat 'You're a freak' as his only possible response
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Stevie literally beats laundry on rocks subverts David's sarcasm by making it factual — a nice inversion. 'You're a freak' is the only correct response and it's delivered with a combination of horror and reluctant affection. Two-word punchline working hard.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, Roland's been having some digestive issues lately, so he won't be joining us for partner yoga.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Honestly, I think everyone's a bit relieved.

jokes.text: Alexis enters David's darkened room: 'Why is it dark? It's like a witch's house in here, David.'
jokes.setup: David has been suffering his panic attack in a darkened room
jokes.punchline: Alexis's witch's house comparison — specific, slightly absurd, and completely her register
jokes.explanation: A light, breezy Alexis observation. 'Witch's house' is a specific enough image to get a smile. Functional character beat as she enters his world.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: So David came in for his appointment today.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? How'd it go?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, let's just say a very tall, very well-dressed man with impeccable hair came in complaining about a rash.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: That's David.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: By tomorrow, everyone in town will know.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Babe, you know that panic attacks aren't real, right? They're just things that celebrities make up to...' David: 'No, I know. I thought so too, but they are real and I'm having one apparently.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is explaining panic attacks to David
jokes.punchline: Alexis repeats David's earlier PR spin theory back at him; David has been corrected by the doctor and now agrees with the medical consensus — reversing their positions
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed callback that rewards attentive viewers. Alexis now holds the exact position David held before his doctor visit, and David has flipped. The role reversal is funny and efficient — both characters' worldviews are equally uninformed but they've swapped. Shows good episodic structure.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not just a woman, I'm a woman who enjoys a nice Pinot Grigio.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not just a woman, I'm a woman who enjoys a nice Pinot Grigiot.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not just a woman, I'm a woman who enjoys a nice Pinto Gregario.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not just a woman, I'm a woman who enjoys a nice Pinto Gringio.

jokes.text: The 'taking a selfish' negotiation — David and Alexis arguing over whose turn it is to be selfish, citing Dubai 2010 as precedent
jokes.setup: Alexis wants to go to yoga; David wants her to stay
jokes.punchline: They have an established system for 'taking a selfish' — selfishness is governed by a turn-based protocol, with historical incidents logged as claims
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest jokes. 'Taking a selfish' as a noun — a formalized turn-based entitlement — is inspired. The concept that the Rose siblings have a protocol governing whose turn it is to be selfish, complete with historical receipts ('Dubai, 2010'), reveals volumes about their upbringing and relationship. It's highly quotable, completely original, and works on multiple levels: as absurdist comedy, character comedy, and family dynamics commentary. The specificity of Dubai 2010 makes it feel like a lived mythology. Justifies 8.5+.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not coming out!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, open the door.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I said I'm not coming out! I'm a failure! I'm a washed-up has-been!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you were never 'up' enough to be washed.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Fine, I will go, but I am not happy about this at all.' David: 'Okay. Get off my bed.' Alexis: 'Ouch, David. Ouch, David!'
jokes.setup: David wins the 'selfish' argument and Alexis agrees to come to yoga
jokes.punchline: Alexis complains about being asked to get off David's bed as if this is the actual injury in the situation
jokes.explanation: A lighter button to the selfish argument. 'Ouch, David. Ouch, David!' is Alexis's way of registering wounded feelings about the dismissal. Gets a mild laugh from the sibling dynamic but doesn't add much beyond punctuating the scene.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I've been having trouble sleeping. I think it's anxiety.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: You know what you need? A good orgasm. That'll help you sleep.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I... that's not really... I mean, I appreciate the suggestion, but—

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Trust me. My husband and I, we do it all the time. Really helps us relax.

jokes.text: Johnny inspecting Moira's outfit on set: 'Is that the outfit you're wearing?' / 'It looks a little less flattering in the daylight.'
jokes.setup: Moira is dressed for the commercial; Johnny and Moira chose this outfit together
jokes.punchline: Johnny critiques the outfit they jointly selected, throwing the choice under the bus now that it's 'in the daylight' — backtracking on shared responsibility
jokes.explanation: The self-implication (he chose it too) makes this more than a standard outfit critique. The 'daylight' excuse is absurd and transparent. A small cringe beat as Johnny keeps inserting himself where he's not wanted.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Okay, so we're going to move into the partner pose now. Just relax and let your body guide you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God! Oh my God, oh my God, oh my God!

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: You okay?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yes, I'm fine. I'm just... very aware of where your hands are right now.

jokes.text: Johnny hijacking the catering: 'Whoa, whoa, cubby, what's this? Who ordered cheese?' then eliminating both the cheese and the melon
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to stay calm before shooting; Johnny keeps finding things to 'fix'
jokes.punchline: Johnny appoints himself food director and immediately vetoes every catering item with executive decisiveness he no longer has the authority to exercise
jokes.explanation: Johnny's compulsion to manage everything — even catering he didn't order — is good character comedy. The word 'cubby' is a funny, slightly condescending address for a crew member he's just met. The 'looks a little soft' assessment of the melon as justification for eliminating it is absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: How did you open the door?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I used my key.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Your key is in your purse.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I used the key from my purse.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Your purse is on the table.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Then I suppose I kicked it open.

jokes.text: Moira to Johnny: 'I know, John. You're so good at trying.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is defending his interference as 'watching your back'
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'You're so good at trying' — a warm-sounding phrase that faintly damns by commending effort rather than results
jokes.explanation: A perfect Moira line. 'You're so good at trying' sounds like a compliment and functions as one within their marriage — it's said with genuine warmth — but it technically praises process, not outcome. The audience hears both the kindness and the subtle assessment. Economy is exceptional — six words carrying enormous weight. High quotability. Justifies 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You know what? I don't think my tongue has ever actually touched the roof of my mouth. I think it just hovers there. Like a little pink cloud.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And if it DID touch, I think I would explode. Into a million pieces. So really, I'm doing everyone a favor by keeping my tongue exactly where it is.

jokes.text: Johnny lingers on the shoot driveway after being sent home — 'You look good. No, keep working please.' — trying to give compliments to the crew who are ignoring him
jokes.setup: Johnny has been sent home but hasn't quite left
jokes.punchline: He hovers, dispensing unprompted compliments to crew who ignore him — a defeated CEO still reflexively trying to manage people
jokes.explanation: A small, sad visual beat. The 'No, keep working please' suggests crew had to stop to look at him in response to the compliment, or he's preemptively telling them not to acknowledge him. Either reading is funny and pathetic in a warm way. Good character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You remember when you did that commercial for the mall? You were brilliant in that.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That was a local spot, Johnny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Exactly. And you nailed it. You can do this too.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was there for all of it. Every take. Every retake. I remember because I had to have a drink after each one.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: After each take?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, and during some of them.

jokes.text: At yoga: 'Okay, I don't think I can do this. There's a lot of people here.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has brought David to a yoga class to help with his panic attack
jokes.punchline: Upon arrival, David's anxiety about the cure is as bad as the original problem
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat — David's anxiety metastasizing into anxiety about the anxiety cure. Less original than earlier panic attack jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, I was thinking about your work on 'The Crows Have Eyes 3', and I realized something. The way you inhabited that role, the commitment you brought to a film where you were primarily reacting to animatronic birds—that's not just acting. That's art. That's the kind of nuanced, layered performance that makes one reconsider the entire medium of daytime television as we know it.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'Normally I do this with Roland, but he had a bad burrito so he's M.I.A. — Trust me, that's good news for everybody.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is partnering with David since Roland can't make it
jokes.punchline: Her enthusiastic 'trust me, that's good news for everybody' — cheerfully implying that Roland's presence at yoga would be worse than his absence
jokes.explanation: A good Roland joke delivered by his wife. The 'bad burrito' suggests a digestive situation that would be especially problematic in a yoga context, and 'good news for everybody' is delivered with a cheerfulness that suggests this is a standard situation. The implication about Roland's yoga presence is rich.

dialogue.speaker: Yoga Instructor
dialogue.line: Where do you feel the most tension in your body?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: My shoulders, mostly. And also I'm deeply afraid that I'm not good enough and that everyone secretly hates me.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: My lower back. And I think my husband might be having an affair.

jokes.text: Mutt to David at yoga: 'Ted told me all about your... Nervous breakdown.'
jokes.setup: David is trying to quietly attend yoga; Mutt is his partner
jokes.punchline: Mutt broadcasts David's 'nervous breakdown' diagnosis (upgrading it from panic attack) with cheerful openness
jokes.explanation: The upgrade from panic attack to 'nervous breakdown' via small-town gossip telephone is good. Mutt's complete lack of tact is characteristic. The pause before 'Nervous breakdown' — as if he's selecting from a menu — is a nice beat.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm Moira Rose, and I'm here to tell you about Herb Ertlinger's Fruit Wines.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm Moira... Roooose. Moira Rose-uh. Moy-RAH. Mo-EE-rah. Mwah-RAH.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Moira... Moira... Moira Rose. Rose Moira. Rose... Moira Rose Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Herb Ertlinger's... Herbie... Herb... Ernie Ertlinger's... Herb Hertlinger's Fruit Wine.

jokes.text: Mutt: 'I was at the cafe. He didn't mention any names, but when he said it was someone "precious," I knew.'
jokes.setup: David is horrified that Mutt knows about his breakdown
jokes.punchline: Ted's descriptor 'precious' was apparently so clearly David that Mutt identified him without a name — which is both a small-town observation and an involuntary character assessment
jokes.explanation: The word 'precious' as David's defining characteristic — communicated through small-town gossip — is both funny and a bit accurate. Mutt saying 'I knew' with the certainty of a detective is a good beat. The implication that David is the only precious person in town makes it specific.

jokes.text: Moira on camera: 'discover a winery that pours care and craftsmanship into every glace.' [pause] 'Oh, I'm sorry. I wanted to say glass, but I... was thinking case.'
jokes.setup: Moira is filming the Herb Ertlinger commercial
jokes.punchline: She blends 'glass' and 'case' into 'glace' — a portmanteau of two wine quantities — and then explains it was a conflation of professional instinct (wanting a case) and the scripted word (glass)
jokes.explanation: The 'glace' mistake is clever because it reveals something true about Moira — she really does think in cases, not glasses. The explanation 'I was thinking case' inadvertently confesses to her heavy relationship with wine. Well-crafted character-based flub.

jokes.text: Moira: 'And if you enjoy flute line as much as—' Director: 'You said "flute line."' Moira: 'Oh. Oh, that is funny.'
jokes.setup: Another take of the commercial
jokes.punchline: Moira says 'flute line' instead of 'fruit wine'; when corrected, her response 'Oh, that IS funny' is the exact wrong response — the self-aware acknowledgment that slows everything further
jokes.explanation: The escalating commercial disasters are a good running gag. 'Flute line' is a funnier mistake than 'glace.' Moira's response 'Oh, that IS funny' — laughing at her own flub rather than correcting course — is perfectly her and slows everything down further. The director's growing exasperation is implied.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Hello, I'm Moina Rose, and if you Li—' Director: 'Okay, back to ones.' Moira: 'Why? Why?' Director: 'You said "Moina."'
jokes.setup: Yet another take of the commercial
jokes.punchline: Moira has now mispronounced her own name on camera
jokes.explanation: The culminating joke of the commercial flub sequence: Moira has now mispronounced her OWN name. This is the perfect escalation of the running gag — the one thing she should absolutely be able to say is her own name, and she can't. The 'Why? Why?' — still not realizing what she said — adds one more beat. 'You said Moina' as the deadpan explanation is delivered with perfect restraint. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Director calls action; long pause; calls action again; another long pause — Moira is so in her head she can't start
jokes.setup: After the name-flub, another take begins
jokes.punchline: Two action calls with no response — Moira has completely locked up. The silence IS the comedy
jokes.explanation: Pure awkward silence comedy. The escalation has now reached the point where Moira can't even begin. Two 'Action!' calls with two silences is beautifully structured. On screen this would be excruciating and funny. The quotability is low because you can't quote a silence, but the impact on a viewing audience would be real.

jokes.text: Thomas the driver summarizing the situation: 'Apparently she just locked herself in the bathroom. He's gonna pull the plug on the whole thing unless, and I quote, "that Bitch gets her Ass out of the trailer."'
jokes.setup: Johnny has been called back to the shoot; Thomas is briefing him
jokes.punchline: Thomas delivers Herb's colorful ultimatum with the neutral affect of someone relaying a weather report — 'and I quote' applying professional precision to profanity
jokes.explanation: 'And I quote' before repeating someone's profane ultimatum verbatim is a reliable comedy structure. The capitalization (the way it's delivered) of 'that Bitch gets her Ass out of the trailer' — clearly Herb at the end of his rope — is funny. Thomas's placid delivery sells it.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Maybe I should get back in the car?' Thomas: 'I'm guessing, yes.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has arrived at a crisis he caused and is now unsure of his role
jokes.punchline: Thomas's 'I'm guessing, yes' — utterly calm, implying Johnny's presence here is probably also a bad idea
jokes.explanation: A small but well-executed deadpan beat. 'I'm guessing, yes' implies Thomas has observed enough of the Rose operation to have calibrated expectations. Three words with good comic timing.

jokes.text: During yoga, Jocelyn tells David to try sex for anxiety. 'Sex puts me right to sleep. You should have lots and lots of sex.'
jokes.setup: David is sharing his anxiety with Jocelyn during the yoga class
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's prescription — loud and cheerful sex recommendation in a public yoga class — delivered with the matter-of-fact confidence of a wellness guru
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's cheerful boundary-free advice is a good character beat. 'Puts me right to sleep' as the primary endorsement is funny (not the sexiest recommendation). 'Lots and lots of sex' with escalating emphasis works as a casual overshare delivered to a near-stranger during yoga.

jokes.text: Alexis and Mutt's increasingly awkward yoga partner stretching — 'I haven't noticed. I have noticed, I haven't — I haven't "noticed" noticed, so...'
jokes.setup: Alexis and Mutt are partnered in yoga; there is clearly some attraction being awkwardly navigated
jokes.punchline: Mutt's verbal gymnastics trying to walk back admitting he noticed Alexis's back — the double denial 'I haven't noticed... I have noticed, I haven't-' is a good fluster
jokes.explanation: A standard flirtation-as-comedy beat, but the specific verbal tripping ('I haven't noticed noticed') is charming. The awkwardness serves the romantic subplot setup.

jokes.text: During a yoga pose where Alexis is holding Mutt's thigh, something goes wrong — 'Let's bring it down... Oh, no! Oh! Mutt should support his own thigh.' Alexis: 'Omigod! Omigod! I'm so sorry.'
jokes.setup: The yoga instructor has Alexis guiding Mutt's thigh; clearly something falls or collapses
jokes.punchline: The yoga instructor's retroactive instruction 'Mutt should support his own thigh' — delivered after the incident, as a correction that's too late
jokes.explanation: A good physical comedy beat — clearly a collapse, fall, or inappropriate contact of some kind. 'Mutt should support his own thigh' is the perfect understated after-the-fact instruction. Alexis's panic spiral 'Omigod Omigod' adds to the cringe. The rewatch bonus is for the implied physical gag.

jokes.text: Moira, locked in the bathroom trailer: 'Oh, I don't know how.' Johnny: 'There's a knob on the door.' Moira: 'No, I mean I don't know how to do this anymore!'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks Moira to let him in; she says she doesn't know how
jokes.punchline: Johnny takes 'I don't know how' literally (the doorknob) before the clarification reveals she meant something existential
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed misdirection that pivots into genuine pathos. 'There's a knob on the door' is the perfect pragmatic response to what he thought was a mechanical problem. The reveal — 'I don't know how to do this ANYMORE' — recontextualizes it as a crisis of professional confidence. The joke works on multiple levels and earns its emotion. Excellent structure. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Johnny asks how Moira opened the bathroom door and she replies: 'I threw a shoe.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has gotten inside the trailer bathroom somehow; he asks how the door opened
jokes.punchline: 'I threw a shoe' — a perfectly matter-of-fact explanation for what is a slightly unhinged action
jokes.explanation: Four syllables, maximum effect. 'I threw a shoe' is one of those lines that becomes a Moira Rose signature — delivered with complete composure in a moment of emotional crisis. The shoe-throwing is both a logical solution (hitting a knob?) and an insane one. Perfect economy — couldn't be shorter. The delivery with no explanation or apology makes it funnier. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Moira: 'This wine is aw-ful! Give me another glass.'
jokes.setup: Moira is stress-drinking in the bathroom trailer
jokes.punchline: She announces the wine is awful while simultaneously demanding more of it
jokes.explanation: A callback to the wine-quality thread — now on set, stress-drinking wine she keeps describing as terrible. The simultaneous condemnation and demand is a good ironic beat. 'Aw-ful' pronounced with Moira's particular vocal cadence would land especially well.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I, I can't feel my tongue. But I know it's there because I'm talking.'
jokes.setup: Moira is drunk on the terrible wine she's been consuming
jokes.punchline: Her logical proof of her own tongue's existence while being unable to feel it — the drunk philosopher
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's top jokes. 'I can't feel my tongue. But I know it's there because I'm talking.' is a tiny drunk-logic syllogism that is completely sound and completely absurd simultaneously. The self-reassurance of the second sentence, which proves the first (if she can talk, she has a tongue) is genuinely clever. It's also extremely Moira — she finds evidence and presents it with theatrical clarity even while intoxicated. Highly quotable, original, perfectly economical. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: Johnny, coaching Moira: 'Moira, remember "Sunrise Bay?" You were drunk most of season three... and half of season four.' [pause] 'End of season five.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to rally Moira by reminding her she's performed professionally while impaired before
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'most of season three' to 'half of season four' with a final 'End of season five' added almost as an afterthought — the cumulative drunk-acting career
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's peaks. The comedic structure is immaculate: start with 'most of season three' (alarming), add 'half of season four' (escalation), pause, then deliver 'End of season five' as a quiet addition that implies the situation was ongoing and well-documented. The rhythm of 'most... half... end of' is a brilliantly constructed escalation. Moira's response 'I'll have to take your word for it' / 'Did I used to have a drinking problem?' adds perfect comic acceptance. This is the kind of backstory revelation that rewards the show's mythology-building. Justifies 9.0.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Did I used to have a drinking problem?' — asked with genuine curiosity
jokes.setup: Johnny has just listed how many seasons of Sunrise Bay she spent drunk
jokes.punchline: Moira asks as a genuine inquiry, not a defensive denial — she's curious about her own past as if it's someone else's story
jokes.explanation: The perfect button to the Sunrise Bay escalation. Moira asking 'Did I used to have a drinking problem?' as a genuine research question — not rhetorical, not defensive — is character comedy at its best. She is currently drunk, asking about past drinking, and treats it as an empirical curiosity. The 'used to' also implies she's categorically unaware of any present-day pattern. Highly quotable and a rewatch bonus for catching the layers.

jokes.text: During yoga's exhale-your-tension moment, Twyla unexpectedly shares: 'I burnt my sausage casserole.'
jokes.setup: The yoga instructor is guiding everyone through a tension-release exercise — 'on a big exhale, breathe it out'
jokes.punchline: Twyla's emotional release is her sausage casserole burning — a completely mundane concern delivered with the weight of genuine grief
jokes.explanation: A brilliant Twyla joke. The guided meditation format creates expectation of emotional revelation; Twyla's is the burnt casserole. The contrast between the spiritual container and the domestic content is perfect. Delivered with apparent sincerity — this IS a real concern for Twyla. Economy is exceptional (five words). It works as a character joke (Twyla's modest, grounded world) and a structural joke (subverting the meditation format). Highly quotable. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Twyla's second exhale confession: 'I'm pretty sure I'm really lonely here.'
jokes.setup: The meditation continues; another tension release
jokes.punchline: Twyla's second share escalates from the casserole to actual existential loneliness, delivered with the same casual register — 'pretty sure' modifying a profound admission
jokes.explanation: The escalation from sausage casserole to existential loneliness in one step is a classic Schitt's Creek move — humor that turns gently sad. 'Pretty sure' is the key word: the uncertainty ('pretty sure' she's lonely) implies she hasn't fully processed it, which is both funny and touching. The contrast with the casserole confession makes this hit harder. Strong economy and character work.

jokes.text: Moira's final commercial take — delivering the Herb Ertlinger copy with full theatrical commitment, mispronouncing the name every single time in new and creative ways
jokes.setup: The emotional arc of the episode has built to Moira getting back up and nailing the commercial
jokes.punchline: She delivers the script beautifully — until the name. 'Herb Erfling...ger. Burt Herngeif. Irv Herb-blinger. Bing Livehaanger. Liveling. Burt Herkurn. Ban-Bingo ling-!'
jokes.explanation: The episode's climactic joke and its best moment. The structure is impeccable: the ENTIRE episode has been building to Moira's comeback moment, and she delivers a genuinely beautiful commercial — and then completely falls apart on the name, generating increasingly unrecognizable versions of 'Herb Ertlinger' in rapid succession. 'Bing Livehaanger. Liveling. Burt Herkurn. Ban-Bingo ling-!' escalates into pure phonemic chaos. The payoff works on every level: it's the payoff of the name-fumbling running gag, it's Moira's glorious imperfect triumph, and it's genuinely hilarious to watch a dignified actress produce 'Ban-Bingo ling' from her name memory bank. One of the episode's peak moments and earns its high score. Justifies 8.7+.

tmdb_overview: Moira is hired to be a local winery's spokesperson;  David struggles with a health problem.
insight: Forty-eight jokes sustain this character-driven episode despite uneven pacing throughout.
